title: Sociological Theories of Stratification
examTip: In evaluation questions, never just describe a theory — apply it and then criticise it using a named opposing sociologist. The strongest answers move between at least two theoretical perspectives with a clear judgement at the end.
introTips: There are five main perspectives on stratification in the AQA spec — make sure you know at least the key argument, key concept and key sociologist for each.
Evaluation is the key skill at the top marks — learn at least two criticisms of each theory.
Postmodernism is often the 'comparison' perspective — it argues the others are outdated, but it is itself heavily criticised.
intro: Why does inequality exist? Is it unavoidable — even useful — or is it a form of exploitation that benefits the powerful at everyone else's expense? How we answer that question divides sociologists more sharply than almost anything else. The five main theories of stratification give very different answers — and understanding them, and their critics, is what separates a good sociology answer from a great one.

keyPoints.heading: Functionalist Perspective on Stratification
keyPoints.content: Davis and Moore (1945), in their paper 'Some Principles of Stratification,' argued that social inequality is both inevitable and functionally necessary for society. Different social positions require different levels of talent and training. The most important positions — those vital for social functioning — must attract the most talented individuals by offering higher rewards. Stratification is therefore meritocratic: people end up where their ability and effort takes them. A doctor earns more than a refuse collector because the doctor's role requires more training, more talent and is more critical to society's functioning. CRITICISMS: (a) Is it really meritocratic? Class background predicts social position more strongly than talent — Pierre Bourdieu showed that cultural and social capital advantage already-privileged groups. (b) Who decides which roles are 'most important'? This is a value judgement, not a scientific fact. (c) Tumin (1953) argued that stratification actually restricts talent — working-class individuals with ability may never develop their potential due to poor education and lack of resources. (d) Some of the highest rewards (inheritance, nepotism, rent) have nothing to do with merit or social function.
writeBullets: Davis and Moore (1945) — inequality is inevitable and functionally necessary
Most important positions must attract the most talented via higher rewards
Stratification is meritocratic — you end up where ability and effort takes you
Tumin (1953) CRITICISM — stratification restricts talent; WC potential goes to waste
CRITICISM — who decides which roles are 'most important'? This is a value judgement
CRITICISM — inheritance and nepotism show high rewards have nothing to do with merit

keyTerms.def: Functionalist sociologists who argued inequality is inevitable and necessary — the most important positions must offer the most rewards to attract talent
A system where people's position is determined by their ability and effort rather than background or birth
Critic of Davis and Moore — argued stratification restricts talent rather than promoting it, because disadvantaged groups cannot develop their potential
The functionalist idea that social arrangements exist because they serve a purpose for the smooth running of society

keyPoints.memTip: Davis and Moore = inequality is necessary to fill important roles. Tumin = wrong, it wastes talent instead.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Tumin (1953) is the classic counter to Davis and Moore — it must appear in any evaluation of functionalism on stratification.

keyPoints.heading: Marxist Perspective on Stratification
keyPoints.content: For Karl Marx (1818–1883), stratification is not functional — it is exploitation that serves the ruling class at the expense of everyone else. Capitalist society is divided into two fundamental classes: the bourgeoisie — the owners of the means of production (factories, land, capital) — and the proletariat — workers who have nothing to sell but their labour power. Marx's concept of surplus value explains exploitation: workers produce more value than they receive in wages, and the difference is taken as profit by the bourgeoisie. Why don't workers revolt? Antonio Gramsci (1891–1937) introduced the concept of hegemony — the process by which ruling-class ideas come to be accepted as natural and common sense, spread through education, religion and the media. This produces false class consciousness: the working class accepts its own subordination. Marx predicted inevitable class conflict and eventually a communist revolution leading to a classless society. CRITICISMS: (a) Communist revolutions have not produced classless societies; (b) Marxism focuses too narrowly on economics and ignores gender and ethnicity as independent systems of oppression; (c) it overemphasises conflict — working-class people in stable democracies have not revolted; (d) Weber argued class, status and party are three separate dimensions.
writeBullets: Bourgeoisie — owners of means of production; proletariat — sell their labour power
Surplus value — workers produce more than their wages; profit is taken by bourgeoisie (exploitation)
Hegemony (Gramsci) — ruling class ideas accepted as natural through education, religion, media
False class consciousness — WC accepts its subordination as inevitable and natural
Marx predicted class conflict and eventual communist revolution → classless society
CRITICISM — ignores gender and ethnicity; Communist states didn't become classless; WC hasn't revolted

keyTerms.def: Marx's term for the capitalist class — owners of factories, land and capital (the means of production)
Marx's term for the working class — those who must sell their labour to survive
The value workers produce above their wages — taken as profit by the bourgeoisie; the mechanism of exploitation
Gramsci's concept — the way ruling-class ideas come to be accepted as common sense, maintaining the status quo without force
Marx's idea that the working class fails to recognise its own exploitation because ruling-class ideology presents inequality as natural
The resources used to produce goods — factories, machinery, land and capital; owned by the bourgeoisie under capitalism

keyPoints.memTip: Bourgeoisie own. Proletariat work. Surplus value = the gap in between = exploitation. Hegemony = why no one revolts.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Ensure students distinguish Marx (surplus value, class conflict) from Gramsci (hegemony, false consciousness) — both are Marxist but different concepts.

keyPoints.heading: Weberian Perspective
keyPoints.content: Max Weber (1864–1920) agreed with Marx that economic position is important, but argued that stratification cannot be reduced to economics alone. Weber identified three independent dimensions of stratification: (a) Class — economic position based on property ownership and market situation; (b) Status — social prestige and honour, which does not always follow class position (an aristocrat may have high status but little money; a successful footballer may have great wealth but lower social prestige in some circles); (c) Party — political power and organised influence over collective decisions. These three dimensions may coincide or diverge. Together they shape life chances (Weber's own concept) — the likelihood of obtaining desirable outcomes. Weber identified status groups — communities sharing social honour and lifestyle (ethnic groups, religious communities) — as important units of stratification alongside economic classes. Weber was more pessimistic than Marx about the possibility of radical transformation — he believed bureaucracy would characterise any complex modern society, communist or capitalist. CRITICISMS: (a) less attention to structural sources of inequality than Marxism; (b) in practice, class usually determines both status and party power — the three dimensions are not truly independent.
writeBullets: Weber — three dimensions: Class (economic), Status (prestige), Party (political power)
Class ≠ Status — an aristocrat may have high status but little money; a celebrity vice versa
Life chances (Weber) — shaped by all three dimensions together, not economics alone
Status groups — communities sharing social honour and lifestyle (ethnic/religious groups)
Weber was pessimistic — bureaucracy persists in any complex modern society
CRITICISM — in practice class tends to determine status and party power; dimensions not truly independent

keyTerms.def: Economic position based on property ownership and market situation
Social prestige and honour — does not always match economic class position
Organised political power and influence — the third dimension of stratification
Communities defined by shared social honour and lifestyle — e.g., ethnic or religious communities
The likelihood of achieving desirable outcomes — shaped by class, status and party together

keyPoints.memTip: Weber's three Ps: Position (class), Prestige (status), Power (party). Together they shape your life chances.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often think Weber just agreed with Marx — push them to articulate the status and party dimensions clearly, with examples.

keyPoints.heading: Feminist Perspective on Stratification
keyPoints.content: Mainstream sociological theory historically ignored gender — most famously, John Goldthorpe (of the Affluent Worker study) used the 'conventional view': classifying women by their husband's occupation, as if women had no independent class position. Feminists challenged this fundamentally, arguing that gender is a primary dimension of stratification as fundamental as class. Patriarchy — systematic male dominance — operates through multiple institutions. Sylvia Walby (1994, 'Theorising Patriarchy') identified six patriarchal structures: paid work, housework, sexuality, culture, male violence, and the state. Different feminist strands offer different analyses and solutions. Liberal feminism (e.g., Betty Friedan, 1963) emphasises legal and institutional reform — the Equality Act 2010 is a liberal feminist achievement. Radical feminism (e.g., Kate Millett, 1970, 'Sexual Politics') argues patriarchy is the fundamental form of oppression from which all others derive — reform is insufficient; the whole system must change. Marxist feminism (e.g., Heidi Hartmann, 1979) argues capitalism and patriarchy are 'dual systems' that reinforce each other — women's unpaid domestic labour benefits both. Black/Postcolonial feminism (e.g., Kimberlé Crenshaw, 1989) argues mainstream feminism centres white, middle-class women's experiences and ignores the intersection of race, class and gender.
writeBullets: Conventional view (Goldthorpe) — women classified by husband's job; feminists reject this entirely
Patriarchy — systematic male dominance across all institutions
Walby (1994) — 6 patriarchal structures: work, housework, sexuality, culture, violence, state
Liberal feminist — reform institutions; Equality Act 2010 (Friedan, 1963)
Radical feminist — patriarchy is fundamental; reform insufficient (Millett, 1970)
Black feminist — mainstream feminism centres white women; intersectionality (Crenshaw, 1989)

keyTerms.def: A system of male dominance operating across all major social institutions
Goldthorpe's approach of classifying women by their husband's occupation — rejected by feminists
Feminist approach that seeks gender equality through legal reform and policy change
Feminist approach (Millett, 1970) that sees patriarchy as the root of all oppression — requiring fundamental social transformation
Approach linking gender oppression to capitalism — women's unpaid domestic labour benefits both capitalism and patriarchy (Hartmann, 1979)
Sociologist who identified six patriarchal structures in 'Theorising Patriarchy' (1994)

keyPoints.memTip: Four feminist strands: Liberal (reform law), Radical (patriarchy is everything), Marxist (capitalism + patriarchy together), Black (add race and class).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often only mention one feminist perspective — encourage them to distinguish and compare at least two for evaluation marks.

keyPoints.heading: Postmodern Perspective on Stratification
keyPoints.content: Pakulski and Waters (1996, 'The Death of Class') argued that class no longer determines identity or life chances in postmodern society. Identity is now constructed through consumption and lifestyle choices: Mike Featherstone summed this up as 'you are what you buy.' Zygmunt Bauman (1925–2017) described 'liquid modernity': social structures are fluid, identities are flexible, and old certainties — class, family, nation — dissolve. Ulrich Beck (1944–2015) argued we now live in a 'risk society': new risks — environmental, health, economic — cross-cut class lines in ways that class analysis cannot capture. CRITICISMS: (a) Mike Savage (2015, Great British Class Survey) found class still highly predicts life chances — the top 10% own 45% of UK wealth, and class background determines educational and health outcomes even if people don't feel 'working class'; (b) postmodernism ignores continuing and growing structural inequalities; (c) not everyone has equal freedom to construct their identity through consumption — the poor are constrained by material circumstances; (d) the 'death of class' thesis ignores the persistence of the super-rich — the top 1% own more than the bottom 50% globally (Piketty, 2014).
writeBullets: Pakulski and Waters (1996) — 'Death of Class': class no longer determines identity or life chances
Identity now built through consumption and lifestyle — Featherstone: 'you are what you buy'
Bauman — 'liquid modernity': social structures fluid, old certainties (class, family) dissolve
Beck — 'risk society': new risks (environmental, health) cross-cut class lines
CRITICISM — Savage (2015): class still predicts life chances despite fluid identities
CRITICISM — poor people cannot freely construct identity through consumption; material constraints are real

keyTerms.def: The view that grand structures like class no longer determine identity — replaced by consumption and lifestyle choices
Postmodern sociologists who argued class is dead as a determinant of identity in 'The Death of Class'
Bauman's term for postmodern society — social structures are fluid, identities are flexible, old certainties dissolve
Beck's concept — modern society defined by new global risks (environmental, health, economic) that cut across class boundaries
Polish sociologist (1925–2017) who coined 'liquid modernity' — the idea that postmodern society is characterised by fluidity and uncertainty
German sociologist (1944–2015) who developed 'risk society' — the idea that new global risks define contemporary life

keyPoints.memTip: Postmodern = class is dead, identity is free. Counter: Savage = class still predicts life chances. The poor can't shop their way to equality.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Postmodernism is the most common perspective to evaluate rather than apply — ensure students can do both.

tables.title: Five Perspectives on Stratification — Comparison Table
rows: Inequality is inevitable and necessary — important roles need the most talented people, attracted by high rewards
Tumin (1953): stratification wastes working-class talent; inheritance shows rewards aren't meritocratic
Stratification is exploitation — the bourgeoisie extract surplus value from the proletariat
Surplus value; hegemony; false class consciousness
Ignores gender and ethnicity; communist states weren't classless; WC hasn't revolted
Stratification has three independent dimensions — class, status and party
Life chances; status groups; three dimensions
In practice class usually determines status and party — dimensions not truly independent
Gender is a primary dimension of stratification; patriarchy operates across all institutions
Which feminism? Liberal, radical, Marxist and Black feminism disagree on causes and solutions
Class is dead — identity is now built through consumption and lifestyle, not class position
Savage (2015): class still predicts life chances; poor people can't freely choose identity through consumption

0.question: What is the main criticism made by Tumin (1953) of Davis and Moore's functionalist theory of stratification?
options: Stratification restricts talent rather than promoting it — working-class individuals with ability may never develop their potential
Davis and Moore ignored the role of status and party in creating inequality
Functionalism only explains inequality in capitalist societies, not socialist ones
Tumin argued that meritocracy does work in practice and Davis and Moore underestimated it
0.correctFeedback: Exactly. Tumin's key point is that stratification does the opposite of what Davis and Moore claim — instead of getting talent to the top, it prevents working-class people with ability from ever reaching their potential.
0.wrongFeedback: Tumin's criticism is specifically about meritocracy. He argued stratification restricts talent — working-class people with ability lack the resources to develop it, so the 'most talented' in the top positions are simply the most privileged, not the most able.

3.question: What does Gramsci's concept of 'hegemony' mean in the context of Marxist theory?
options: The process by which ruling-class ideas come to be accepted as natural and common sense, preventing the working class from recognising its exploitation
The physical force used by the ruling class to prevent workers from organising
The economic advantage that the bourgeoisie hold over the proletariat through ownership of the means of production
The idea that working-class culture is inferior to ruling-class culture
3.correctFeedback: Correct. Hegemony explains why the working class doesn't revolt — ruling ideas (spread through education, media and religion) present inequality as natural, producing false class consciousness.
3.wrongFeedback: Hegemony is about ideas, not force. Gramsci argued that the ruling class maintains power primarily through ideology — making its ideas seem like common sense — rather than through direct coercion.

4.question: Which sociologist produced the Great British Class Survey (2015) and what did it find?
options: Mike Savage — class still strongly predicts life chances, and identified 7 classes rather than 3
Pakulski and Waters — class has died as a meaningful social category, replaced by consumption
Zygmunt Bauman — social structures are liquid and class identity is completely fluid
Max Weber — status and party are more important than class in determining life chances
4.correctFeedback: Mike Savage's Great British Class Survey (2015, 161,000 respondents) directly challenged the postmodern 'death of class' thesis by showing class still powerfully predicts life chances, and found 7 distinct classes including the precariat.
4.wrongFeedback: The Great British Class Survey was led by Mike Savage (2015). It directly challenged Pakulski and Waters by showing class STILL strongly predicts life chances — and found 7 classes, not just 3.

commonMistakes: Describing theories without evaluating them — AQA questions on theory almost always require criticism using a named opposing sociologist.
Confusing Marx and Weber — both analyse class, but Weber added status and party as independent dimensions and rejected the idea that revolution was inevitable.
Saying postmodernism means inequality doesn't exist — Pakulski and Waters argue class IDENTITY has declined, not that material inequality has disappeared.
Treating 'feminist perspective' as a single view — liberal, radical, Marxist and Black feminists disagree substantially on causes and solutions.
Forgetting Tumin (1953) when evaluating functionalism — he is the key named critic of Davis and Moore in the AQA specification.
examTips: Learn the five-perspective comparison table — for any 'evaluate a sociological perspective' question, you can reach for it immediately.
The strongest evaluation answers move between at least two perspectives: e.g., Marxism describes exploitation, but feminists say it ignores gender, and Weber says class alone is too simple.
Named sociologists with dates earn marks — Davis and Moore (1945), Tumin (1953), Gramsci, Walby (1994), Crenshaw (1989), Savage (2015) are all frequently rewarded.
For postmodern questions, Savage (2015) is the best single counter-argument — class still predicts life chances even if identity is fluid.
revisionCardBullets: Functionalism — Davis and Moore (1945): inequality necessary to fill important roles with talented people
Meritocracy — system where position reflects ability and effort
Tumin (1953) — criticism: stratification wastes working-class talent, not meritocratic
Marxism — bourgeoisie (own) exploit proletariat (work) through surplus value
Hegemony (Gramsci) — ruling ideas accepted as natural, producing false class consciousness
Weberian — three dimensions: class (economic), status (prestige), party (political power)
Weber — life chances shaped by all three dimensions, not economics alone
Feminism — patriarchy is a primary dimension of stratification (Walby, 1994: 6 structures)
Liberal feminist: reform law. Radical feminist (Millett, 1970): patriarchy is fundamental
Intersectionality (Crenshaw, 1989) — race, class and gender compound each other
Postmodern (Pakulski and Waters, 1996) — class dead; identity through consumption and lifestyle
Savage (2015) counters — Great British Class Survey: class still predicts life chances; 7 classes found
